pages.title: Welcome to Phishing & Email Safety
pages.subtitle: Learn how phishing works and how to spot it before damage happens.

pages.title: What is phishing?
pages.text: Phishing is a social engineering attack where someone pretends to be a trusted source to trick you into clicking a link, opening an attachment, or sharing sensitive information. Social engineering means manipulating people into revealing confidential information or performing unsafe actions. These messages are designed to look legitimate and often target people through email, text messages, or fake websites. The goal is usually to steal passwords, deliver malware, or gain access to internal systems.

image.alt: Example of a phishing email (replace later)

pages.title: Types of phishing
pages.text: Phishing can happen through multiple communication channels. Some common types include: • Email phishing – Fake emails pretending to be from companies, coworkers, or vendors. • Smishing – Phishing messages sent through SMS or text messages. • Vishing – Phone calls where attackers impersonate IT staff, banks, or government agencies. Even if the message seems legitimate, always verify before responding or clicking anything. Smishing Example impersonating IRS.

image.alt: Examples of email, SMS, and phone phishing

bullets.title: Phishing emails can look almost identical to real messages
bullets.text: Attackers often copy logos, tone, formatting, and even email signatures to appear legitimate.

bullets.title: Some phishing attacks use look-alike domains
bullets.text: For example, micr0soft.com instead of microsoft.com — a difference that’s easy to miss.

bullets.title: Business Email Compromise (BEC) is common
bullets.text: Attackers impersonate a boss or vendor to request money, wire transfers, or gift cards.

bullets.title: Urgency is a major red flag
bullets.text: Messages like "act now" or "final notice" are designed to pressure you into mistakes.

bullets.title: Did you know?
bullets.text: According to research data by Statista, the number of phishing sites has more than doubled during the Pandemic and while it has come down from top, recent indicates that the numbers are rising again.

pages.title: Common phishing red flags
pages.text:
Most phishing attempts include warning signs if you know what to look for.

Be cautious of:

• Urgent or threatening language
• Requests for passwords or MFA codes
• Unexpected attachments or links
• Sender addresses that don’t quite match
• Messages that pressure you to act quickly

When in doubt, pause and verify before taking action.

video.caption: How to identify phishing red flags
Breakdown of a realistic phishing attempt (replace later)

pages.title: What to do if you spot phishing
pages.text: If you believe a message is phishing: 1) Do not click links or open attachments 2) Do not reply to the message 3) Report the incident using your organization’s reporting process 4) Delete the email after reporting Reporting helps protect not just you, but everyone in your organization.

video.caption: Example of how phishing reporting works
